domains.domain_context_preview: ## Data collection and analysis The focus groups and telephone interviews were audiotaped and fully transcribed. Data analysis was informed by qualitative methodology (Pope and Mays, 2000). Individual questions or information needs were identified from the transcripts using the participants' own language where possible. These were discussed by both facilitators to ensure consistency of interpretation and were organised into categories to facilitate comprehension, as suggested by Ley (1988). Further focus groups and/or telephone interviews were conducted until no additional topics were raised. A draft QPL was developed using the categories and questions identified in the transcripts. The draft QPL was then reviewed by relevant health professionals and their suggestions were incorporated. The document was further refined to ensure ease of understanding and a Flesch–Kincaid reading grade...

domains.domain_context_preview: ## Data collection and analysis Prior to the consultation, patients completed a questionnaire regarding their information and involvement (decisional control) preferences, and anxiety levels. General preferences for information were assessed using a question from the Cassileth Information Styles Questionnaire measuring the type of information preferred (Cassileth et al, 1980). Preferences for decisional control were assessed using a validated and reliable question from previous studies in cancer patients (Sutherland et al, 1989; Degner et al, 1997b). Patients were classified as wanting an ‘active’, ‘passive’ or ‘collaborative’ role when making decisions about treatment. Patient anxiety was measured by the Spielberger State Anxiety Inventory (Spielberger, 1983) (20 items), which produces a continuous score (range 20–80) with higher values representing higher anxiety levels. The latter is...

domains.domain_context_preview: ## Enrolment and interventions From August 2003 to April 2005 pregnant women were enrolled at the MDH ANC if their gestational age was ≤ 28 weeks, they did not report allergies to sulpha drugs, and they were permanent residents of the CISM study area. After written informed consent was obtained, the lowest available study number was assigned. A computer-generated sequential list contained the study numbers linked to treatment identification letters, randomly ordered in blocks of 10. Tablets of SP or placebo, identical in shape and colour, were stored in 10 bottles labelled only with a single treatment identification letter. Women were randomised to receive 3 tablets of SP (1500 mg sulphadoxine/75 mg pyrimetamine) or placebo. The project health nurse administered study drugs to women with at least 12 weeks of gestational age. Doses were given twice from the second trimester, at least one...

domains.domain_context_preview: ## Study design This double blind, individually randomised, placebo controlled trial had the primary objective of estimating the additive protective effect of two-dose IPTp with SP to that of ITNs on LBW prevalence. Based on previous estimates in the area, a LBW prevalence of 20% and an estimated 25% reduction to 15% in the presence of ITNs [14], 411 women per group were needed to show a lack of difference between the two groups with a confidence interval of 7 (8% to 22%), at the 5% level of significance with a 80% statistical power. The study protocol was approved by the National Mozambican Ethics Review Committee, and the Hospital Clinic of Barcelona Ethics Review Committee. --- ## Methods The protocol for this trial and supporting CONSORT checklist are available as supporting information; see Checklist S1 and Protocol S1. --- ## Enrolment and interventions From August 2003 to April...

domains.domain_context_preview: ## Methods The protocol for this trial and supporting CONSORT checklist are available as supporting information; see Checklist S1 and Protocol S1. --- ## Study design This double blind, individually randomised, placebo controlled trial had the primary objective of estimating the additive protective effect of two-dose IPTp with SP to that of ITNs on LBW prevalence. Based on previous estimates in the area, a LBW prevalence of 20% and an estimated 25% reduction to 15% in the presence of ITNs [14], 411 women per group were needed to show a lack of difference between the two groups with a confidence interval of 7 (8% to 22%), at the 5% level of significance with a 80% statistical power. The study protocol was approved by the National Mozambican Ethics Review Committee, and the Hospital Clinic of Barcelona Ethics Review Committee. --- ## Enrolment and interventions From August 2003 to April...

domains.domain_context_preview: ## Enrolment and interventions From August 2003 to April 2005 pregnant women were enrolled at the MDH ANC if their gestational age was ≤ 28 weeks, they did not report allergies to sulpha drugs, and they were permanent residents of the CISM study area. After written informed consent was obtained, the lowest available study number was assigned. A computer-generated sequential list contained the study numbers linked to treatment identification letters, randomly ordered in blocks of 10. Tablets of SP or placebo, identical in shape and colour, were stored in 10 bottles labelled only with a single treatment identification letter. Women were randomised to receive 3 tablets of SP (1500 mg sulphadoxine/75 mg pyrimetamine) or placebo. domains.domain_context_preview: ## RESEARCH DESIGN AND METHODS Allocation to the study group was concealed from the study coordinator and the participant until after the recruitment phone call. Following initial oral consent, the study coordinator consulted the allocation assignment table. Participants were randomly assigned in equal numbers to the two groups. The study's statistician used a computer random number generator to create a random number table in a nonblocked sequence. For participants in the intervention group, the study coordinator arranged for in-person, follow-up written consent and an intake visit. Participants in the intervention and usual-care groups did not receive additional incentive for participation in the study. Baseline data for all participants were from automated data in the electronic medical record. --- ## RESEARCH DESIGN AND METHODS Between August 2002 and May 2004, we enrolled...

domains.domain_context_preview: ## RESULTS The initial screening criteria for age and diabetes diagnosis were met by 709 patients, of whom 626 were excluded before and during the phone screen: 355 had GHb <7%, 82 were non–English speaking, 28 lacked computer access, and 19 declined participation (Fig. 1). Those excluded were not significantly different from those enrolled by sex (49.5 vs. 47.8% male, respectively), race (80 vs. 75% nonwhite) or age (59.4 vs. 57.9 mean years of age). Among the 83 individuals randomized to the usual-care and intervention groups (Table 2), the difference in percentage of Caucasians between groups approached significance (73.0% usual care vs. 89.7% intervention group; P = 0.06); 54 participants (65%) had all three diagnoses of diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and hypertension. Six individuals in the usual-care group and three individuals in the intervention group did not have follow-up GHb...

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Front Health care limited to clinic visits does not meet the needs of many patients with diabetes. Care systems that use Web-based communication provide an opportunity to shift the focus in health care away from the office and toward patients’ daily lives at home. Patient interaction with online care plans and electronic medical records may further enhance the effectiveness of chronic care (1,2). Little is known, however, about the impact of using Web communications and shared electronic medical records in the primary care of patients with diabetes. --- ## Front We present the results of a randomized trial examining a Web-based diabetes support program that aimed to improve glycemic control for patients with type 2 diabetes. The program consisted of access from home to the electronic medical record, secure electronic communications between patients and providers, and interactive...

ground_truth_support: "Allocation to the study group was concealed to the study coordinator and the participant until after the recruitment telephone call“ (p. 234)

domain_rows.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel: All outcomes
domain_rows.support_text: Quote: "Participants, physicians, and care managers could not be feasibly blinded to group assignment after randomization." Comment: Blinding of participants and personnel was not done and the outcomes (e.g. glycemic control via care-manager actions, medication adjustments, and behaviors) are likely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Probably not done.

support_context.quote: Participants, physicians, and care managers could not be feasibly blinded to group assignment after randomization.
support_context.relevance: Direct statement that participants and personnel were unblinded, relevant to performance bias.

support_context.quote: The care manager ... adjusted hypoglycemic medications, and conferred with the primary care provider as needed.
support_context.relevance: Intervention involved active management and clinician behavior that could be influenced by knowledge of allocation, affecting outcomes.

domain_rows.reasoning: The trial was explicitly open-label with participants and care providers unblinded, and the intervention required clinician and participant behavior (care management, medication adjustments) that could materially affect outcomes (glycemic control), so lack of blinding likely introduced performance bias.
ground_truth_support: Objective outcomes No blinding HbA1c, total cholesterol, blood pressure, healthcare consumption Unclear whether differences in performance influence HbA1c, blood pressure, or total cholesterol
Patient‐reported outcomes Empowerment

domain_rows.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment: All outcomes
domain_rows.support_text: Quote: "Study staff collecting outcomes were not blinded to group assignment." Comment: Probably not done — outcome assessors were unblinded and primary outcome (GHb) collection and follow-up could be influenced by knowledge of allocation (timing of tests, reminders, or handling of missing data), creating detection bias.

support_context.quote: "Study staff collecting outcomes were not blinded to group assignment."
support_context.relevance: Direct statement that outcome assessors were unblinded.

support_context.quote: Participants were called 12 months after randomization for a GHb test if one had not been obtained between 9 and 12 months postrandomization.
support_context.relevance: Outcome timing and follow-up were managed by study staff who were unblinded, allowing differential ascertainment.

domain_rows.reasoning: Outcome assessment was performed by unblinded study staff and the primary outcome (GHb) required staff-managed follow-up and timing; this makes measurement likely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation, so risk of detection bias is high.
ground_truth_support: Objective outcomes HbA1c, total cholesterol, blood pressure. Study staff collecting outcomes were not blinded (p. 236), but no impact on lab results
Patient‐reported outcomes Empowerment

domain_rows.support_text: Quote: "Six individuals in the usual-care group and three individuals in the intervention group did not have follow-up GHb measurements." Comment: Missing outcome data were small (9/83 ≈11%), reasonably balanced across groups (6 vs.3), and the authors performed intent-to-treat analyses and multiple sensitivity imputations (baseline observation carried forward, group averages, etc.) which did not change effect size — therefore risk of bias from incomplete data is likely low.

support_context.quote: Six individuals in the usual-care group and three individuals in the intervention group did not have follow-up GHb measurements.
support_context.relevance: Shows amount and balance of missing primary outcome data by group.

support_context.quote: Sensitivity analyses included a single imputation method... baseline observation carried forward... and an average of all available post GHb by study group. The different approaches for imputing missing GHb data did not change effect size.
support_context.relevance: Indicates handling of missing data and robustness of results to imputation.

domain_rows.reasoning: Missing primary outcome data were modest in proportion (~11%), reasonably balanced between arms, and sensitivity analyses using multiple imputation approaches did not alter the effect estimate, so bias from incomplete outcome data is unlikely to materially affect results.
ground_truth_support: Objective outcomes Low loss to follow‐up, sensitivity analyses with imputation methods (p. 236)
Patient‐reported outcomes Low loss to follow‐up, sensitivity analyses with imputation methods (p. 236)
